stories_covered: Developer TAM sizing: 100-150M developers at $5K per year = $500B-$1T opportunity
Coding tool competition: Cursor vs Lovable vs Replit vs Microsoft vs Anthropic
Defensibility debate in horizontal AI products
Seed-stage investing in competitive AI spaces
harry_takes: The TAM for developer tooling is genuinely massive—at 100-200M developers paying $5K annually, you get a half-trillion to trillion-dollar market
Cursor's $30B valuation is justifiable when you model 100% developer penetration, even at conservative per-user spend assumptions
Defensibility doesn't exist at seed stage; the only reliable predictor is team quality, execution speed, and first-to-market advantage
At the seed stage, founders must accept they cannot build defensible moats and instead must race to scale before incumbents or other startups catch up
harry_boldest_quote: If you're not seeing massive TAM expansion, there's just no point in even playing as VCs.
predictions: Tom: Cursor captures 40-60% market share within 5 years
Tom: Microsoft bundling will win enterprise, Anthropic will own 20% via Claude integration
Lemkin: Replit v3 is so good it disrupts established leaders; another player will emerge within 18 months that blows everyone out of the water
Lemkin: Once you've switched to an AI coding agent, you'll never go back—creates strong retention
disagreements: Tom was skeptical about disruptive challengers lasting once enterprise locks in; Lemkin argued the pace of AI improvement is so fast that new players can still emerge and win despite Cursor's lead.
harry_role: Harry drove the TAM sizing discussion and forced clarity on what the real addressable market should be. He moderated between Tom's enterprise-stickiness thesis and Lemkin's disruption-is-fast thesis, keeping focus on market size as the ultimate justification for valuations.
notable: The group calculated that if 100M developers pay $400-500/month for coding assistance, it justifies valuations of $500B+ for a single player, making the $30B raise seem conservative despite looking expensive on revenue multiples.
